peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and low agreeableness, combined with strong libertarian and GAL values, lead to mild support for prioritizing national sovereignty as a check on supranational overreach, despite cosmopolitan leanings.

High traditional-authoritarian-nationalist and authority-submission scores, combined with a dangerous-worldview and strong ingroup-loyalty, drive a firm prioritization of national sovereignty, though moderate openness and a cosmopolitan orientation prevent maximal endorsement.

High authoritarianism, ethnic nationalism, and collective narcissism combined with very low openness and agreeableness drive strong prioritization of national sovereignty over supranational authority.

Despite very low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation scores, high libertarian skepticism of state control and strong anti-elitism populism create a moderate preference for national sovereignty as a check on distant supranational bodies, though tempered by very high openness and low ingroup loyalty.

High populist-people-centrism (72.08) and popular-sovereignty (82.38) combined with low authoritarian-submission (53.73) and high GAL-TAN green-libertarian (87.01) values suggest a strong preference for local democratic control over nationalistic priority, but moderate civic-nationalism (68.13) tempers this into mild opposition.

High authoritarian submission, strong national identification, and very high dangerous-worldview beliefs drive a firm prioritization of national sovereignty to protect the ingroup from external threats.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low economic left-wing orientation and high conscientiousness with distrust of institutions (low trust in government) drive clear opposition to shared fiscal capacity, seen as inefficient and risky.

Very high libertarian anti-state-control and anti-taxation progressivity, coupled with low political trust and a competitive-jungle worldview, produce clear opposition to shared fiscal capacity as a threat to individual and national autonomy.
Extreme economic left-wing preferences for redistribution clash with deep distrust of external institutions and a competitive worldview, producing firm opposition to shared fiscal authority.

Extremely low economic left-right redistribution support (21.35) combined with high conscientiousness and order values produce clear opposition to shared fiscal transfers, though very high agreeableness and fairness moral foundation slightly soften the rejection.

High fairness-reciprocity (97.4) and equality (68.81) paired with strong social-dominance anti-egalitarianism (71.96) create a tension that resolves into moderate support for shared fiscal capacity as a practical tool for reducing inequality, despite high group-based dominance (93.2) making it a cautious endorsement.
Very low trust in institutions and strong anti-elitism, combined with a competitive-jungle worldview, make sharing fiscal authority with supranational bodies seem risky and illegitimate.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate nationalism and high collective narcissism, tempered by high openness, result in mild opposition to deeper integration, valuing national identity over supranational structures.

Extreme traditional-authoritarian-nationalist and ethnic-nationalism scores, combined with a dangerous-worldview and high authoritarian submission, generate strong opposition to supranational integration as a dilution of national identity and security.
Very high authoritarian submission, conventionalism, and ethnic nationalism, alongside very low openness and libertarianism, yield strong categorical rejection of supranational integration.

Very high openness to experience and global identity (16.72) pull toward mild support, but extremely low cosmopolitan orientation (7.52), high authoritarianism (97.4), and strong anti-elitism populism create a countervailing skepticism that leaves this person mildly opposed on balance.

Very high GAL-TAN green-libertarian mean (87.01) and pluralism-diversity support (91.34) combined with low cultural-traditionalism (38.03) and high cosmopolitan orientation (62.96) drive clear support for supranational integration as a means to foster cooperation and address global challenges.
High ethnic nationalism and strong conventionalism oppose ceding decision-making power to external entities, though moderate openness to experience prevents outright maximal rejection.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High trust in science and experts, combined with pragmatic conscientiousness, yields mild support for common defense as a practical solution, despite reservations about sovereignty.

A dangerous-worldview and high punitive attitudes create mild support for a common defense as a pragmatic security measure, but strong nationalism and low trust in external institutions temper enthusiasm.
Moderate support for national order and unity is outweighed by high authoritarian law-and-order preferences and distrust of external institutions, leading to mild opposition to shared defense.

High trust in government (91.13) and police (90.81) combined with very high authoritarian law-and-order preference (90.7) and a dangerous worldview (18.91, low) produce mild support for pooled defense as pragmatic, though low nationalism and high libertarianism limit enthusiasm.

Moderate dangerous-worldview (20.23) and low threat-sensitivity (32.39) reduce security concerns, while high anti-elitism (75.04) and moderate trust in government (71.95) create a cautious, mildly supportive stance for pooled defense as a practical but not fully trusted supranational institution.
Very high authoritarian aggression and a dangerous-worldview support collective defense against shared threats, but low trust in supranational institutions tempers enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high openness and strong GAL values, along with low authoritarianism, drive clear support for free movement, reflecting a progressive and globalist outlook.

Extremely high restrictionist-outgroup-policy preference and ethnic-nationalism, combined with a dangerous-worldview and high social-dominance orientation, drive near-maximal opposition to free movement as a threat to national cohesion and security.
Extreme ethnic nationalism, restrictionist outgroup policy preferences, and a dangerous worldview drive very strong opposition to open borders.

Extremely high openness to experience (93.73), very high cultural openness (6.52 is low but reversed), strong pluralism/diversity support (94.76), and personal experience as a temporary resident with a student visa strongly incline toward supporting free movement.

Very high GAL-TAN pluralism-diversity (91.34) and civil-liberties orientation (67.87) combined with low restrictionist-outgroup-policy (63.49) and high cosmopolitan orientation (62.96) produce strong support for open borders, tempered only by moderate ethnic-nationalism (56.26) which prevents maximal endorsement.
Extremely high restrictionist outgroup policy preference and ethnic nationalism produce strong opposition to open borders, seen as a threat to cultural and national integrity.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low agreeableness and high authoritarian submission (RWA) create ambivalence: respect for legal authority clashes with distrust of external institutions, leading to near-neutral stance.

Very high libertarian skepticism of state control and low trust in judiciary, paired with strong authority-submission to national institutions, produce clear opposition to a regional court overriding domestic legal authority.

High system justification and deference to legitimate authority are directed at national institutions, while very low political trust in external bodies and high collective narcissism produce clear opposition to regional court authority.

High trust in judiciary (76.21) and strong fairness/reciprocity moral foundation (93.67) are outweighed by very high libertarian skepticism of state control (93.67), anti-elitism populism (96.41), and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style that resists external authority.

High anti-elitism (75.04) and low trust in judiciary (74.21) relative to high trust in science (70.41) create ambivalence; the person's strong populist-people-centrism (72.08) and moderate authoritarian-submission (53.73) yield a near-neutral stance with a slight lean against regional court authority as potentially elitist.
Very low trust in the judiciary and strong populist sovereignty beliefs make accepting a regional court's authority feel like an illegitimate imposition on the people's will.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and trust in experts favor harmonization for efficiency, but low economic leftism and libertarian skepticism of state control keep support mild and cautious.

High libertarian anti-regulation and anti-state-control, combined with moderate openness and a preference for national order, yield mild-to-moderate opposition to shared standards as an unnecessary constraint on national flexibility.
Strong preference for national order and unity, combined with high conventionalism and low openness, leads to clear opposition to harmonized regulatory standards imposed by supranational bodies.

Very high conscientiousness (87.81) and order value (93.49) favor harmonized standards for efficiency, but high libertarian anti-surveillance (95.49) and strong anti-elitism populism create ambivalence, resulting in near-neutral mild support.

High fairness-reciprocity (97.4) and low authority-submission (25.69) combined with moderate system-justification (57.06) and high political-efficacy (89.91) support shared standards as a practical, efficiency-enhancing measure, though high group-based dominance (93.2) and anti-egalitarianism (71.96) keep support from being strong.
Moderate openness to experience and some cosmopolitan orientation allow mild openness to harmonized rules, but strong system justification and conventionalism lean toward preferring local control.
